name: Dosh
definition: refers to threshing using a utensil designed for that purpose, or treading on the produce.
body_lines: was performed by treading or using a flail to beat produce in order to remove the seeds. As such, the
refers to threshing using a utensil designed for that purpose, or treading on the produce.
is the removal of foods or liquids from where they are encased.
Although this would seem to include most fruits and vegetables that we peel, like bananas and oranges, that is not the case. The threshing in the

was done while the produce was still in the field. Most of our fruits and vegetables are peeled just before they are eaten, and would in fact be rotten if peeled so far in advance. Since the manner is incomparable, it is not included in the
. Moreover, the peels are directly attached and can be considered part of the fruit itself. Therefore, the removal of their peels is no different than cutting the fruit, which is obviously permitted.
One should be careful not to use a peeler on
, however, and use a knife instead.

name: Zoreh
definition: refers to the act of removing chaff from grain, using a tool to throw produce into the air, whereby the wind blows the lighter chaff away from the heavier

into the air, whereby the wind blows the lighter chaff away from the heavier

to the separation of foods and non-foods, specifically using the wind, as it
maintain that wind is not required, and Zoreh refers to the separation of food
from non-food, specifically at the stage when the kernels are still together
with their chaff. All later refinements of the food are not included in Zoreh,

name: Losh
definition: Kneading invariably involves two steps: a) Pouring the liquid into the flour; b) Mixing the flour and water together to make a dough. There is a Talmudic dispute regarding what exactly defines
body_lines: encompasses all activities which, like kneading, join small particles into one mass using liquid, and applies to both foods and non-foods. It also includes later treatment of dough, like basting or braiding.
Kneading invariably involves two steps: a) Pouring the liquid into the flour; b) Mixing the flour and water together to make a dough. There is a Talmudic dispute regarding what exactly defines
Rebbi says that just pouring the liquid over the flour is already
since the flour and water fuse on impact. Kneading them together is also Biblically forbidden, but as an independent, additional transgression. Rabbi Yosi Bar
, however, says that one only transgresses

by mixing the ingredients together and not by merely pouring the liquid over the flour. While some halachic authorities, most notably Sefer Hateruma, follow Rebbi’s interpretation, the vast majority side with Rabbi Yosi.

There are a variety of substances that can be kneaded with liquid. Some, like fine flour, completely mix with water to the extent that the flour becomes a different entity—dough. These substances are halachically termed “

.” There are other substances which are not kneadable, like oats or finely diced vegetables. When these are mixed with water they just clump together but don't transform into a new entity. These are called “
.” It is apparent that Rabbi Yosi’s ruling applies to
substances. The authorities debate, however, what he would say about kneading

name: Gozez
definition: , and involves cutting or uprooting hair, nails, or anything else that grows from a person, animal, or bird.
body_lines: , and involves cutting or uprooting hair, nails, or anything else that grows from a person, animal, or bird.
is only transgressed if one uses an instrument meant for that purpose, like a pair of scissors or a nail clipper.
Pulling out hair with one’s hand is not the way hair is typically removed and therefore not prohibited by

Likewise, biting one’s nails is not included, since it is not the typical way to cut one’s nails. Nevertheless, the Sages prohibited these activities because they closely resemble the Biblical prohibition.
In a scenario where pulling out hair with one’s hand is normal, however, like tweezing (which is considered using one’s hand), doing so is considered Biblically forbidden.
is prohibited even after the skin has been removed from an animal.
As such, one should not pull out fur from a fur coat.
Cutting away dead skin, warts, or pimples is also forbidden under

name: Melaben
body_lines: but the dispute is merely academic, because all agree that any activity which cleans or refines fabric is included in
The question is merely which activity is the
, “With regard to clothing (or any absorbent material) soaking is considered laundering.”

is transgressed simply by soaking unclean cloth in water, even without scrubbing or removing a stain, and b) soaking is only problematic with absorbent materials like clothes, but does not apply to non-absorbent materials like rubber or leather.
Practically, this means that if a garment has dirt or a stain on it, one may not put any liquid at all
on the stain in order to treat it.

name: Tzad
body_lines: . While trapping most creatures is definitely rabbinically
is only transgressed if certain conditions are met:
The trapper must intend to benefit from the
animal. The general rule with Biblical prohibitions on
only transgressed if one intends to benefit from the forbidden action itself,
not just to offset something else. So one who traps an insect because it was
annoying, for example, only transgresses a rabbinic prohibition.
The creature must be of a species that is
usually trapped by people because it has some sort of use. This would exclude
person traps one of these insects for some purpose, he is still not liable.
The animal must be trapped in a small
enough space that it would be easy for one to grab it from there.

The skin of cooked chicken, however, may be removed. This is because cooking

name: Boneh
definition: As mentioned, the melacha of Boneh involves assembling and building a permanent structure. The Sages extended the prohibition to include the creation of temporary structures out of concern that it may lead to making a permanent structure.
body_lines: Assembling and building a permanent structure is forbidden on
This includes adding to an existing structure as well as leveling the ground to make it more usable.
encompasses a wide range of forbidden activities and proper study of the subject will ensure that one observes its laws properly. Here, we will explore some of the basic principles.

were made from specially designed planks of wood that were inserted into sockets on the ground and connected to each other. The construction of these walls and the roof that was stretched over them are the source for the
, whose construction serves as the source for all forbidden activity on
, was a land-based structure. Therefore, the
of Boneh forbids creating any land-based, permanent structures. This includes fences, posts, houses and shelters. Paving a path with tiles
or digging a hole in the ground to store things
is also considered Boneh, as one is making the space suitable for a constructive purpose.

name: Mechabeh
definition: appliances is also forbidden on Shabbat, but
body_lines: Extinguishing a flame is forbidden on
Since the 39 general categories of forbidden labor on
are modeled after their sources in the
, it follows that the Biblical prohibition of
is limited to extinguishing a flame to serve a useful purpose, as

. For example, some have the custom to light the wicks of the Shabbat candles on Friday afternoon so that they will be easier to light at candle-lighting time. This would be an example of extinguishing for a useful purpose. Practically, there are few examples of this in everyday life, and most applications of
are thus rabbinic prohibitions based on the ruling of the Sages, who extended the
to include all extinguishing, even when not done for a useful purpose.
Most Rabbinic authorities say that causing a filament to glow by turning on an electric appliance is an act of
(kindling), and turning the appliance off is an act of

name: Hotzaah
definition: Carrying or transferring any item from one place to another—under certain conditions—constitutes the
body_lines: Carrying or transferring any item from one place to another—under certain conditions—constitutes the
Its multiple layers and facets, combined with the many rabbinic prohibitions that extend from it, make the
It is particularly challenging because even scenarios that seem innocuous can fall into the category of
. For example, if you live in an apartment building, you may not carry the garbage into the hallway to throw it down the chute without a specific type of
(which will be discussed below). People may be aware that an
to shul or push a baby’s stroller, but connecting that to taking a few steps into the hallway of one’s own building does not seem like a natural progression.
, for example, takes something that was previously raw and renders it edible.
